data.content_ref: inline:{"claim": "The simultaneous collapse resulted from a critical threshold breach in the international tin-copper supply chain; the loss of a single, obscure tin source (e.g., from Afghanistan or Central Asia) caused a bronze shortage that delegitimized warrior elites whose power depended on monopoly access to metal weaponry, triggering internal revolts.", "mechanism": "Resource bottleneck leading to legitimacy crisis: the inability to produce bronze arms broke the social contract between the palace and the military caste, allowing marginalized groups to overthrow the hierarchy.", "scope": {"covers": ["Mycenaean Greece", "Hittite Anatolia", "Ugarit"], "excludes": ["Egypt", "Iron-working peripheral groups"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "Evidence of abundant bronze stockpiles in destroyed palace layers dating to the final decade of occupation", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "Extends the supply chain hypothesis found in 83eb... by specifying the political mechanism of elite delegitimization rather than just economic contraction."}
